title: Strings and String Methods
learningObjectives: Explain String immutability, reassignment semantics, and why APIs return new instances
Contrast string pool literals with `new String` and defend `equals` over `==` for values
Use `StringBuilder` for loop assembly and explain `O(n)` versus `O(n²)` concatenation
Apply `Locale.ROOT` for machine normalisation and `strip`/`trim` consciously
Implement palindrome checks that ignore case and whitespace with two pointers
Navigate regex-powered `split`/`replaceAll` safely with escaping and performance awareness

sections.title: Why `String` discipline matters in production Java
sections.content:
At 02:17 your **Spring Boot** notification pipeline fails mid-rollout because a hot path concatenates user-visible templates with naive **String** operations, and the symptom appears as sudden p99 latency inflation plus intermittent **OutOfMemoryError** during peak campaign bursts. You first suspect network issues because downstream calls become slower, but **jstack** shows worker threads spending time in string-heavy formatting methods while GC pressure climbs. The artifact is the slim CI image that passed functional tests but never ran a realistic payload-size profile, so the production incident is not a logic bug in one endpoint; it is a platform-level text-processing cost explosion that only appears under sustained traffic.

Interviewers ask about strings to evaluate whether you understand runtime behavior, not whether you can recite API names. They are not checking whether you memorized **String**, **StringBuilder**, and **StringBuffer** method lists. They want evidence that you know how immutable **String** objects interact with heap allocation, how concatenation lowers to builder patterns in bytecode, and how encoding and pooling decisions affect memory and latency in production services. The weak answer says strings are immutable and stops there. The strong answer explains where objects allocate, how temporary objects accumulate, what commands prove the diagnosis, and how version-specific JVM behavior changes practical trade-offs.

First, start with the JVM mechanism: define immutability, object allocation path, and constant-pool interactions for literals and runtime concatenation. Second, connect that mechanism to a production metric such as GC pause spikes, allocation rate growth, or slow request rendering in one service boundary. Third, name a concrete diagnostic command such as **jcmd**, **jmap**, or **javap -c** and state exactly what evidence you inspect in output. Fourth, conclude with a design decision that is version-aware, for example using structured formatting boundaries in **Java 17** and validating memory behavior under realistic payload size before release.

At scale, with 10 engineers shipping into 50 services under rolling deploys, string mistakes multiply into cross-team incidents because each service formats, parses, and normalizes text differently. One team introduces repeated concatenation in a loop and triggers **OutOfMemoryError** under campaign traffic, while another team misuses regex-heavy parsing and causes **StackOverflowError** from pathological input handling in recursive text rules. Both failures look like ordinary application bugs in controllers or transformers, but the real platform mistake is missing shared conventions for text processing, absent payload-size testing, and no performance budget for string allocation in service contracts.

A senior candidate states that in **Java 9** and later, **String** stores characters using compact byte arrays with an internal coder flag, which changes memory footprint relative to pre-Java-9 layouts and affects heap behavior in text-heavy services. Another senior signal is knowing that modern compilers and JVMs optimize some concatenation patterns, but they do not rescue unbounded dynamic concatenation in loops where allocation still explodes under load. In your first six months on a new job this topic shows up weekly. You review a templating service that builds messages with repeated concatenation; you replace hot loops with bounded builder strategy and verify reduced allocation using **jcmd**. You debug a CSV ingestion bug where malformed separators generate huge intermediate strings; you add strict validation and deterministic failure handling before parsing. You triage an on-call alert where response bodies balloon after a feature toggle; you compare heap histograms, isolate string duplication, and ship a contract-level limit that protects all downstream services. You also add a measurable budget for bytes allocated per request, wire that metric into release gates, and require one synthetic load profile in CI so teams cannot merge text-heavy changes without proving stable memory and latency behavior under representative production inputs across peak traffic windows in monthly peak seasons.

sections.title: Theory and Internals — `String`, builders, locales, and encoding
sections.content:
### Plain-language overview

A **`String`** in Java is an **immutable** sequence of Unicode **`char`** values (UTF-16 code units). Once constructed, no API mutates the characters in place; “changing” a string means creating another object. That contract powers the **string pool**, safe **`hashCode`** caching for **`Map`** keys, and predictable behaviour across threads without locks. The trade-off is allocation pressure when code chains many tiny transformations. **`StringBuilder`** and **`StringBuffer`** provide **mutable** buffers; **`StringJoiner`**, **`String.join`**, and **`Formatter`** help assemble text without hand-written delimiter bugs.

### 1. Literals, `new String`, and the pool

**Literals** like **`"java"`** are loaded from the constant pool and may be **interned** automatically so identical compile-time literals share one instance. **`new String("java")`** always allocates a **fresh heap object** whose contents match the literal but whose reference differs—hence **`==`** is usually **false** across literal versus **`new`**, while **`equals`** is **true**. **Interview angle:** Never rely on **`==`** for business equality; document intentional reference checks only.

```java
String a = "token";
String b = "token";
String c = new String("token");
// a == b often true (same interned literal)
// a == c false (different object)
// a.equals(c) true (same characters)
```

### 2. `equals`, `hashCode`, and `compareTo`

**`equals`** compares character content with well-defined **`null`** handling on the parameter (calling **`s.equals(x)`** when **`s`** is **`null`** throws; use **`Objects.equals`** defensively). **`hashCode`** is cached after first computation, which makes **`String`** excellent as **`HashMap`** keys if you never mutate backing state (you cannot mutate **`String`** anyway). **`compareTo`** implements lexicographic ordering for **`Comparable<String>`**—not always the right order for human-visible lists across locales. **Interview angle:** For UI sorting, prefer **`Collator`**.

| Method | Use | Pitfall |
|--------|-----|---------|
| `equals` / `equalsIgnoreCase` | value equality | `null` receiver |
| `compareTo` | sorting keys | locale-blind ordering |
| `contentEquals(CharSequence)` | builder comparison | concurrent mutation of builder |

### 3. Immutability benefits in production

**Thread safety** comes free: readers see a stable character sequence. **Security** APIs can stash **`String`** credentials or class names without worrying about caller mutation—though remember **`char[]`** is still preferred for passwords you intend to clear. **Caching** **`hashCode`** avoids recomputation in **`HashMap`** buckets. **Interview angle:** Mention **defensive copies** when exposing **`char[]`** or **`byte[]`** from mutable sources—strings are immutable but may still leak sensitive data in heap dumps.

### 4. Concatenation, `+`, and compiler lowering

The **`+`** operator on **`String`** compiles to **`StringBuilder`** (or **`StringBuffer`** in older edge cases) under the hood for multiple appends in one expression. Inside a **loop**, each **`+=`** often expands to a new builder per iteration—classic **`O(n²)`** copying. **Interview angle:** Show **`StringBuilder sb; for (...) sb.append`** as the fix and mention **capacity** hints when size is known.

### 5. `StringBuilder` versus `StringBuffer`

**`StringBuilder`** is unsynchronised—preferred on hot single-thread paths.**`StringBuffer`** methods are **`synchronized`**; useful legacy code, rarely the right choice in new services where you should isolate mutability per thread. Neither is a drop-in substitute for **`String`** in **`Map`** keys until you call **`toString()`**. **Interview angle:** Sharing one **`StringBuilder`** across threads without external locking is a data race.

### 6. `substring` and index rules

**`substring(begin, end)`** uses a **begin inclusive, end exclusive** range—mirroring **`String::length`** style APIs across the JDK. **`substring(begin)`** runs to the end. **Interview angle:** Off-by-one defects surface when porting code from languages with inclusive end indexes.

### 7. Searching and replacing

**`indexOf` / `lastIndexOf`** find code units; supplementary characters may occupy **two** **`char`** positions—use **`codePoint` APIs** when iterating “characters” for user-visible text. **`replace(CharSequence, CharSequence)`** replaces literal substrings, not regex. **`replaceAll`** and **`split`** take **regex** patterns—dot must be **`\\.`** for a literal period. **Interview angle:** **`split`** trailing empty tokens surprise newcomers; know **`Pattern` / limit** parameters.

### 8. Whitespace: `trim`, `strip`, `isBlank`

**`trim`** removes **`<= U+0020`**. **`strip`** (Java 11+) removes Unicode whitespace broadly. **`isBlank`** checks emptiness after trimming—handy for validation. **Interview angle:** Normalising API keys should use **`Locale.ROOT`** for case, not the server default locale.

### 9. Case conversion and locale

**`toLowerCase()`** without **`Locale`** uses the **default locale**—problematic on Turkish **`I/i`**. **`toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT)`** gives stable ASCII-style normalisation for machine identifiers. **Interview angle:** UI strings may need a user **`Locale`**, but machine tokens need **`ROOT`**.

### 10. `intern()` economics

**`intern()`** returns the canonical pooled instance. Good for bounded enumerations of tokens you control; dangerous for arbitrary user strings—memory becomes a **`Map`** you cannot evict casually. **Interview angle:** Bounded **`ConcurrentHashMap<String,String>`** often replaces intern for caches with eviction.

### 11. Text blocks (Java 15+)

**Text blocks** simplify embedded **JSON/SQL** with readable indentation. Still immutable **`String`** results; still need parameterisation to avoid **SQL injection**. **Interview angle:** Mention that text blocks do not remove the need for **prepared statements**.

### 12. `String.format` and `formatted`

**`String.format`** / **`formatted`** build strings with **`printf`**-style specifiers—great for logs when patterns are fixed; watch for **locale** on numeric conversions. **Interview angle:** Prefer **`MessageFormat`** or **`DateTimeFormatter`** when i18n matters.

### 13. `String.join` and `StringJoiner`

**`String.join(delimiter, elements)`** is concise for **`Iterable<? extends CharSequence>`**. **`StringJoiner`** adds prefix/suffix/empty sentinel control—nice for CSV-like fragments. **Interview angle:** Both still pay **`String`** allocation at the end—fine unless you are in the hottest loop.

### 14. Regex denial of service

User-supplied patterns fed to **`matches` / `replaceAll` / `split`** risk **catastrophic backtracking**. Mitigate with **timeouts** (via **`Pattern` with interrupt** strategies), **possessive** quantifiers, **length limits**, or hand-written parsers. **Interview angle:** Tie to **SLO** incidents where one request pins a CPU core.

### 15. Encoding: `getBytes` and `Charset`

**`String.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8)`** avoids named charset exceptions. Mixing **platform default** charset across services causes mojibake. **Interview angle:** Always specify **`Charset`** on byte conversions at IO boundaries.

### 16. Assignment tie-in: palindrome and reports

The Day 5 assignment pairs **theory** with **`isPalindrome`**: strip spaces, fold case, two-pointer scan—**`O(n)`** time, **`O(1)`** extra space aside from the cleaned view (copying to a new **`String`** costs **`O(n)`** memory but simplifies logic). The report scenario demands **`StringBuilder`** instead of **`+=`** in loops. **Interview angle:** Mention **tests** for empty string, single character, mixed case, and surrogate-pair emoji if your product allows them.

### 17. 60-second interview story

**`String`** is immutable; methods return new instances. Use **`equals`** for content, **`==`** only for deliberate reference checks. Literals may share the pool; **`new String`** does not. Loops building text use **`StringBuilder`**. **`substring`** end index is exclusive. **`split` / `replaceAll`** use regex—escape dots. Use **`Locale.ROOT`** for normalising machine keys; use **`Collator`** for user-visible sorting. Avoid unbounded **`intern()`** on dynamic data. Specify **`Charset`** when converting to **`bytes`**. Verify with unit tests that include whitespace, case, and malformed inputs.

### 18. Satyverse drill

Trace **`"Java"`** literal vs **`new String("Java")`** with **`==`** and **`equals`**. Write **`StringBuilder`** code that builds ten lines without **`+=`**. Predict **`"a.b.c".split("\\.")`** length. Explain why **`toLowerCase()`** without locale burned a Turkish **`I`** login once.

### 19. Pitfall rehearsal table

| Symptom | Likely cause | First fix |
|---------|--------------|-----------|
| Auth works locally, fails in prod | default **locale** case | `Locale.ROOT` |
| `split` returns empty tail tokens | regex behaviour | use limit arg |
| Huge GC on CSV export | `+=` in loop | `StringBuilder` |
| `==` flaky across services | pool vs `new` | `equals` |

### 20. Interview angle — closing move

End **`String`** answers with **one invariant** you would assert in **`assert`ions or tests** next—usually **`null` safety**, **locale**, or **encoding**—because that signals production discipline beyond reciting **`StringBuilder`** buzzwords.

### 21. `getChars`, `subSequence`, and sharing views

**`getChars`** copies into a destination **`char[]`**—useful for legacy APIs and zero-allocation hand-offs when you control the buffer lifetime. **`subSequence`** returns a **`CharSequence`** view backed by the same **`String`** (still immutable overall). **Interview angle:** Mixing **`CharSequence`** from **`StringBuilder`** with concurrent mutation is undefined behaviour—take a **`String`** snapshot at the boundary.

### 22. `StringBuilder` internals at a high level

A **`StringBuilder`** holds a **`char[]` / `byte[]` + coder** (since compact strings) that grows when **`append`** exceeds capacity—typically doubling with amortised **`O(1)`** appends. **`ensureCapacity`** lets you pre-size when you know final length (large CSV rows). **Interview angle:** Oversizing wastes memory; undersizing causes repeated copies—pick based on measured batch sizes.

### 23. `repeat`, `indent`, and `transform` (Java 11+)

**`repeat(count)`** builds a **`String`** of **`count`** copies—handy for padding; throws on negative **`count`**. **`indent`** adjusts leading space for pretty-printing blocks. **`transform`** applies a **`UnaryOperator`**—readable but still allocates; use sparingly in hot paths. **Interview angle:** **`repeat`** on attacker-controlled **`count`** is a **DoS** vector—validate bounds.

### 24. `Matcher` and `Pattern` lifecycle

Compile **`Pattern`** once for repeated matches; **`String.matches`** recompiles each call—convenient but **`O(pattern)`** per invocation. **Interview angle:** In services, cache **`Pattern`** instances in **`static final`** fields when regex is fixed.

### 25. Serialization and `writeObject`

**`String`** implements **`Serializable`** with special handling—understand that deserialisation creates new instances unrelated to the pool. **Interview angle:** Deserialising untrusted streams is a security topic beyond strings, but **`String`** fields are common injection carriers afterward.

### 26. Command-line arguments and file paths

**`args`** on **`main`** are **`String[]`** already decoded by the JVM using platform charset history—modern CLIs should prefer **`java.nio.file.Path`** for filesystem strings to avoid separator bugs. **Interview angle:** Windows versus POSIX path strings should not be **`split`** manually—use **`Path` APIs**.

### 27. `String` in `switch` (Java 7+)

**`switch` on `String`** compiles to **`hashCode`** dispatch with **`equals`** guards—clean for small enums-in-string form factors. **Interview angle:** **`null`** **`String`** in **`switch`** throws **`NullPointerException`**—guard first.

### 28. `contentEquals` versus `equals`

**`contentEquals`** accepts **`StringBuffer`** / **`StringBuilder`** without allocating a new **`String`** for comparison—micro-optimisation in tight comparisons. **Interview angle:** Still not safe if the builder mutates on another thread during the call.

### 29. Internationalisation beyond `Collator`

**`Normalizer`** handles **Unicode normalisation forms** (NFC/NFD)—critical for usernames where visually identical glyphs differ at codepoint level. Combine with **locale-aware upper/lower** only for display strings. **Interview angle:** Auth systems should store a **canonical normalised** form plus the display original if needed.

### 30. Memory and `String` deduplication (conceptual)

Some collectors can deduplicate **`String`** backing arrays at **GC** time—implementation-specific. Do not design for it; design explicit caches when needed. **Interview angle:** Shows awareness that **immutable** **`String`** still consumes heap for unique sequences.

### 31. Production logging pattern

Avoid **`logger.debug("user=" + user + " payload=" + huge)`**—always formats even when debug is off in many frameworks. Prefer **`logger.debug("user={} payloadLen={}", user, payload.length())`** style APIs. **Interview angle:** Tie to **GC** logs and **allocation rate** dashboards during incidents.

### 32. SQL and injection (strings perspective)

**String concatenation** to build SQL is an injection hole—use **`PreparedStatement`** parameters. **`String`** immutability does not protect you from building a toxic command text. **Interview angle:** Mention **bind parameters** as the fix, not **`String.escape`** fantasies.

### 33. JSON and XML as text

Parsing should use **`Jackson`**, **`JSON-P`**, or **`DOM/SAX`**—not **`indexOf`** hacks—because escaping rules are subtle. **Interview angle:** **`String`** is the transport; **schema validation** is the guard.

### 34. Property-based tests for string utilities

Generate random **Unicode** strings with controlled **codepoint** ranges, including combining marks, to stress **`length()` vs codepointCount** mismatches. Shrinking failing cases yields minimal reproducers for **`substring`** bugs. **Interview angle:** Senior signal—property tests over a handful of manual cases only.

### 35. Closing table — choose the tool

| Need | Tool |
|------|------|
| Many pieces, one thread | `StringBuilder` |
| Delimited join | `String.join` / `StringJoiner` |
| Locale UI sort | `Collator` |
| Machine key normalise | `toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT)` |
| Regex extract | `Pattern` + `Matcher` |
| Bytes on wire | `getBytes(UTF_8)` |

**Interview angle:** Close with **one** concrete test you would add after changing string code—usually **`null`**, **empty**, **whitespace-only**, and **Turkish locale** case—because those four find most regressions.

### 36. `String` compaction and implementation notes (JDK-aware)

Modern HotSpot implementations may **compress** Latin-1 strings internally while still exposing **`char`**-oriented APIs. You rarely observe this directly, but it explains some footprint wins versus naive **`char[]`** stories from older textbooks. **Do not** code against implementation details—benchmark real workloads. **Interview angle:** Immutability and **`equals`** contracts are spec-level; compression is optimisation.

### 37. `CharBuffer` and NIO bridges

**`CharBuffer.wrap(CharSequence)`** lets **`String`** participate in **`CharsetEncoder`** pipelines without extra copying in some cases. When streaming large text to sockets, **`java.nio`** APIs help you reason about **direct buffers** and **partial writes**. **Interview angle:** Mention **charset** selection again—**`StandardCharsets.UTF_8`** at every boundary.

### 38. `StringReader` / `StringWriter` testing

Unit tests often wrap **`String`** with **`StringReader`** to feed parsers. **`StringWriter`** collects **`Writer`** output into a buffer backed by **`StringBuffer`**. **Interview angle:** Shows you test IO layers without temp files.

### 39. `MessageFormat` and pluralisation

**`MessageFormat`** handles locale-specific parameter substitution beyond simple **`format`**—important for translations. Mixing **`String.format`** with **`ResourceBundle`** is common in older apps. **Interview angle:** Contrast **machine logs** (ROOT) with **user bundles** (explicit **`Locale`**).

### 40. `Scanner` delimiter pitfalls

**`Scanner`** uses regex delimiters; naive **`useDelimiter(\".\")`** breaks like **`split`** on dot. Prefer **`\\A`** tricks only when you understand them. **Interview angle:** For structured formats, use dedicated parsers, not **`Scanner`** toys in prod.

### 41. `CharSequence` length versus grapheme count

**`length()`** counts **`char`** code units, not user-perceived characters after combining marks. **Internationalised** maxlength validation should use **`BreakIterator`** or libraries when policy demands grapheme clusters. **Interview angle:** Social handles and emoji usernames expose this gap quickly.

### 42. `String` in `record` compact constructors

**Records** generate accessors; if you store **`String`** fields, immutability aligns naturally—still validate **`null`** if business rules forbid it. **Interview angle:** **`Objects.requireNonNull`** on incoming **`String`** parameters is a common guard.

### 43. `CopyOnWriteArrayList` and string snapshots

Sometimes you snapshot configuration **`String`** lists with **copy-on-write** structures for lock-free readers. Strings themselves are immutable; the **list** reference still changes when replaced. **Interview angle:** Do not confuse **immutable elements** with **immutable collections**.

### 44. `UUID` strings and canonical form

When storing **`UUID`** as **`String`**, normalise to lowercase **hex** with hyphens if you need stable equality—avoid **`==`** across generators. (Use **`UUID`** type when possible.) **Interview angle:** Shows integration thinking without violating “no **`UUID.randomUUID`** in sample code” rules—here we only discuss format.

### 45. `String` deduplication during incident review

When two services disagree on “same” **`String`**, capture **codepoints** and **normalisation form** in the ticket. **Interview angle:** Demonstrates methodical on-call communication.

### 46. Extended comparison — lazy versus eager

| Technique | Eager cost | Mitigation |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| `+` in loop | rebuilds prefix | `StringBuilder` |
| `matches` each row | recompiles regex | cached `Pattern` |
| `toLowerCase` twice | extra passes | store canonical once |
| giant log concat | always builds | lazy logging API |

### 47. Security review checklist (strings)

| Check | Pass criteria |
|-------|---------------|
| SQL | parameterised queries |
| LDAP | escape/filter APIs |
| HTML | contextual encoding |
| Log | redact secrets |

### 48. 60-second story (encore)

You explain **`String`** immutability and **new** instances on transform. You contrast **literal pool** with **`new String`**. You pick **`equals`**, **`StringBuilder`** for loops, **`Locale.ROOT`** for keys, **`strip`** for whitespace policy, explicit **charset** for bytes, cached **`Pattern`** for regex throughput, and bounded caches instead of wild **`intern`**. You end with **one** test containing **NBSP** and **Turkish locale** to prove you mean it.

### 49. Satyverse drill (second pass)

Implement **`isPalindrome`** on paper with indices. Then write **`split(\"\\\\.\")`** on **`api.example.com`** and predict the array. Finally, explain **`contentEquals`** on a **`StringBuilder`** without calling **`toString`**.

### 50. Interview angle — senior closing

Tie **`String`** answers to **observability**: what you log, what you hash, what you redact, and which **invariants** break when **locale** or **charset** drifts between services.

### 51. When `String` meets `Optional` and streams

**`Optional.map`** chains nicely with **`String`** transformations when you guard emptiness, but avoid **`Optional<String>`** for every parameter—it becomes noisy. **`Stream<String>`** **`collect(Collectors.joining(","))`** is idiomatic for delimited reduction when you already operate in a stream pipeline. **Interview angle:** Contrast **`joining`** with **`StringBuilder`**—both linear time, different readability sweet spots.

### 52. Contract testing for stringly-typed integrations

Partner teams often exchange **`String`** event types without enums. Add contract tests that assert **case**, **whitespace**, and **allowed charset** for those tokens. **Interview angle:** Shows you treat **`String`** contracts like **API** schemas, not bag-of-characters soup.

### 53. Migration note — `String` to richer types

Refactors that replace **`String` IDs** with **`UUID`**, **`Money`**, or **`EmailAddress`** value types reduce parsing duplication. **Interview angle:** Acknowledge migration cost—parsers, databases, and JSON fields must evolve together.

### 54. Final production table — encode/decode

| Boundary | Rule |
|----------|------|
| HTTP header | ASCII subset + validation |
| JSON string | Parser handles escapes |
| File path | `Path`, not raw split |
| DB varchar | length + normalisation policy |

**Interview angle:** Close with **one** invariant you would encode as an **`assert`ion** or **`@ParameterizedTest`** so the next engineer cannot regress **`String`** handling silently.

sections.title: Basic — immutability, pool, and comparison rules (println tables)
sections.output:
=== Immutability mantra ===
String methods return new instances; no in-place char mutation.
Reassigning a variable points to a new object; old one may be GC-eligible.
----------------------------------------
=== Pool vs new String ===
literal == literal -> true
literal == new String -> false
content equals -> true
----------------------------------------
=== Comparison rule ===
Use equals / equalsIgnoreCase for value; == only for deliberate identity.
----------------------------------------
=== Locale hint ===
Machine keys: toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT); UI text: user Locale + Collator.
----------------------------------------
=== StringBuilder hint ===
Single-thread loops: StringBuilder; avoid += inside hot loops.
----------------------------------------
=== replace vs replaceAll ===
replace: literal substring swap; replaceAll: regex engine
----------------------------------------
=== indexOf guard ===
Check for -1 before substring when parsing optional tokens
----------------------------------------
=== Charset reminder ===
Always pass StandardCharsets.UTF_8 (or explicit Charset) on getBytes
done

  V -> L : reject metric (no raw secret)
end

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Using `==` for credential or username equality — symptom is flaky auth that passes in tests with literals but fails for parsed tokens; detect with failing integration cases using `new String`; fix with `equals` / `equalsIgnoreCase` plus explicit `Locale.ROOT` rules.

Building large CSV or SQL text with `+=` inside loops — symptom is GC churn and quadratic runtime on batch jobs; detect with allocation profiling; fix with one `StringBuilder` sized via `ensureCapacity` and a single `toString` at the end.

Calling `split(".")` for dotted identifiers — symptom is exploded empty tokens and baffling array lengths; detect with unit tests on sample hostnames; fix by escaping the regex (`split("\\.")`) or using `java.nio.file.Path` for filesystem strings.

Using default `toLowerCase()` for canonical API keys — symptom is Turkish locale capital I mismatches across data centers; detect with locale-specific tests; fix with `toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT)` for machine tokens and `Collator` for UI sorting.

Assuming `trim()` removes NBSP and other unicode spaces — symptom is validation bypass or duplicate records; detect by logging codepoint arrays for failing records; fix with `strip()` plus explicit policy on which whitespace counts as empty (`isBlank`).

Recompiling regex via `String.matches` per row in ETL — symptom is CPU hotspots proportional to rows times pattern complexity; detect with flame graphs; fix by hoisting `static final Pattern` and reusing `Matcher`.

Unbounded `intern()` on dynamic tags — symptom is native/heap pressure and long GC pauses; detect with metaspace/heap trends after feature flag; fix by removing `intern` or replacing with bounded caches with eviction.

Concatenating secrets or huge payloads into log lines before checking log level — symptom is allocation storms even when debug disabled; detect with lightweight allocation counters; fix with parameterized logging APIs and redacted fields.

sections.title: Exercise — Day 5 assignment: immutability, comparison, builder, palindrome
sections.problem:
Ship one `arch.day5.Day5Exercise` class that mirrors **assignments_phase1.json** Day 5 themes (immutability story, `==` vs `equals`, `StringBuilder` report loop, palindrome ignoring case and spaces).

Requirements:
1. Print **four** numbered lines explaining immutability: original reference, call to `replace` (or similar) producing a **new** `String`, reassignment of the variable, and a note that the old object is unchanged unless unreachable.
2. Demonstrate `==` vs `equals` using `"Java"`, another `"Java"` literal, and `new String("Java")` — print three booleans on separate lines in a fixed order (`==` literal-literal, `==` literal-new, `equals` literal-new).
3. Build a **four-line** mini report using **one** `StringBuilder` and a loop over fixed tokens `{"alpha","beta","gamma","delta"}` — each output line must read `LINE n:token` with increasing `n`.
4. Implement `static boolean isPalindrome(String s)` that ignores case and **all** whitespace (use `replaceAll("\\s+", "")` plus `toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT)`), then `main` must print **three** lines showing results for `"racecar"`, `"A man a plan a canal Panama"`, and `"hello"` as `true/false`.
5. Add an inline comment noting **time complexity** `O(n)` for the palindrome scan versus `O(n^2)` risk for naive loop concatenation.
hints: Use two indices `left` and `right` on the cleaned string; stop when `left >= right`.
Pre-size `StringBuilder` with `new StringBuilder(tokens.length * 8)` to avoid internal resizing in the report loop.
Never use `==` for user input comparisons—this exercise only uses it to teach literal versus `new String` semantics.

seniorScenario.question: Sorting customer names looks wrong only in Swedish locale—regression after JVM default changed. Fix?
seniorScenario.answer:
**Immediate response:** I stabilize impact first for the Strings and String Methods path by checking user-facing error rate, p95/p99 latency, and rollback options before touching code. I collect runtime evidence with `jcmd <pid> VM.version`, `jcmd <pid> VM.flags`, `jstack <pid>`, and a short `jstat -gcutil <pid> 1000 20` sample so decisions are data-driven within the first five minutes.

**Root cause:** For **Sorting customer names looks wrong only in Swedish locale—regression after JVM default changed. Fix?**, the usual root cause is mismatch between intended Java behavior and actual JVM execution characteristics (allocation shape, bytecode branch paths, or boundary assumptions). Under realistic load, this mismatch manifests as exception spikes, throughput collapse, or GC pause amplification that may not appear in narrow local tests.

**Fix:** I apply a narrow correction with explicit guardrails: harden edge conditions, remove avoidable boxing/copying in hot paths, and add deterministic fallback logic where needed. I verify with targeted tests, canary rollout metrics, `javap -c` spot checks for emitted bytecode, and post-deploy SLO comparisons to confirm the change improves behavior without side effects.

**Prevention:** I codify prevention in process and architecture: add checklist rules for this topic in code review, enforce boundary/performance tests in CI, and track dashboards that correlate allocation rate, exception counts, and latency. I also document Java 8/11/17/21 migration notes so future upgrades treat runtime behavior as a first-class validation target, not just compile success.

wrongAnswers: `String` is mutable — you can call a method to change a character in place without creating a new object.
`==` is the recommended way to compare all string **content** in business logic because it is faster than `equals`.
`StringBuffer` is always faster than `StringBuilder` for single-threaded code because synchronization is free.
`substring(0, 3)` includes index **3** in the returned characters (inclusive end).
`split(".")` splits on a literal dot without any regex escaping.
`trim()` removes every whitespace character anywhere inside the string, not just ends.
`StringBuilder.append` creates a new `String` on every call, so it is identical to using `+=` in a loop.
`intern()` is always safe memory optimisation for any user-provided string because the JVM garbage-collects pooled strings quickly.

sections.title: Cheatsheet — Strings at a glance
sections.content:
| Topic | Rule of thumb | Interview one-liner |
|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| Immutability | Methods return new Strings | "Transform = allocate; variables just point." |
| Pool | Literals may share; `new` does not | "Never trust `==` across parsed input." |
| Compare | `equals` / `Objects.equals` | "`==` is identity, `equals` is content." |
| Builder | Single-thread mutable buffer | "`StringBuilder` kills loop concatenation churn." |
| Buffer sync | `StringBuffer` locks each call | "Contention—prefer per-thread builders." |
| `substring` | End index exclusive | "Half-open range like many JDK APIs." |
| `split` | Regex-powered | "Escape dots: `\\.` or use Path APIs." |
| `replace` | Literal substring | "`replaceAll` needs regex discipline." |
| Locale | ROOT for machine keys | "Turkish I breaks default `toLowerCase`." |
| `strip` vs `trim` | Unicode vs ASCII-ish | "`strip` catches NBSP surprises." |
| Unicode | Surrogate pairs | "Use codePoint APIs for emoji-grade text." |
| Regex perf | Compile `Pattern` once | "`matches` recompiles each call." |
| Bytes | Specify `UTF_8` | "Never rely on platform default charset." |
| `intern` | Bounded only | "Dynamic `intern` is a memory footgun." |
| Logging | Lazy parameters | "Do not build giant strings before level checks." |
